critique.summary: The article reports that GitHub has been experiencing frequent outages and service disruptions, including issues with Actions, pull requests, notifications, and Copilot. It highlights that GitHub's uptime has fallen below 90% at times, which is significantly lower than the industry gold standard of 99.999% uptime, and notes that GitHub's SLA guarantees 99.9% uptime only for Enterprise Cloud customers.
mainPoints: GitHub experienced multiple service issues on February 9 and 10, 2026, affecting Actions, pull requests, notifications, and Copilot.
Notification delays of up to 50 minutes were reported, and Copilot faced policy propagation problems preventing access to new models.
GitHub's public status page was changed, making it harder to assess overall uptime over the past 90 days.
Unofficial reconstructed status data suggests GitHub's uptime dropped below 90% at one point in 2025.
GitHub's SLA guarantees 99.9% uptime only for Enterprise Cloud customers, not all users.
The article contextualizes GitHub's issues within a broader trend of cloud service instability, noting that some vendors struggle to maintain even 90% uptime.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about GitHub's outages and service disruptions are consistent with publicly available incident reports and status updates from GitHub around the dates mentioned. GitHub's SLA for Enterprise Cloud customers does specify 99.9% uptime, and it is known that public status pages sometimes change in format, affecting transparency. The assertion that uptime dropped below 90% in 2025 is based on unofficial reconstructed data and should be treated cautiously. Overall, the article's factual claims about outages and uptime challenges are accurate, but some data relies on unofficial sources.
considerations: The article does not provide direct official statements or multiple sources confirming the exact uptime percentages or outage durations beyond GitHub's own status updates.
It does not explore the technical reasons behind the outages or GitHub's plans to improve reliability.
The impact of these outages on different user segments (e.g., free vs. enterprise users) is not detailed.
No perspectives from GitHub or Microsoft representatives are included to provide context or mitigation efforts.
The article does not compare GitHub's uptime with other major cloud or code hosting providers in detail.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on GitHub's recent reliability and security challenges, with many users attributing outages to infrastructure migration, increased load from AI usage, and security flaws. Several comments highlight the complexity of maintaining high availability at scale, while others express frustration with perceived neglect of core platform stability in favor of new features like AI.

comments.author: cedws
comments.text:
While GitHub obsess over shoving AI into everything, the rest of the platform is genuinely crumbling and its security flaws are being abused to cause massive damage. Last week Aqua Security was breached and a few repositories it owns were infected. The threat actors abused widespread use of mutable references in GitHub Actions, which the community has been screaming about for years, to infect potentially thousands of CI runs. They also abused an issue GitHub has acknowledged but refused to fix that allows smuggling malicious Action references into workflows that look harmless.

GHA can’t even be called Swiss cheese anymore, it’s so much worse than that. Major overhauls are needed. The best we’ve got is Immutable Releases which are opt in on a per-repository basis.
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed and plausible security issues corroborated by known incidents, reflecting a deep understanding of GitHub Actions vulnerabilities.

comments.author: jedberg
comments.text:
GitHub is in a tough spot. From what I've heard they've been ordered to move everything to Azure from their long standing dataceners. That is bound to cause issues. Then on top of that they are using AI coders for infra changes (supposedly) which will also add issues.

And then on top of all that, their traffic is probably skyrocketing like mad because of everyone else using AI coders. Look at popular projects -- a few minutes after an issue is filed they have sometimes 10+ patches submitted. All generating PRs and forks and all the things.

That can't be easy on their servers.

I do not envy their reliability team (but having been through this myself, if you're reading this GitHub team, feel free to reach out!).
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully connects infrastructure migration and AI-driven changes to operational challenges, aligning with known risks of large-scale platform transitions.

comments.author: swisniewski
comments.text:
To be honest, I’m not surprised that GitHub has been having issues.

If you have ever operated GitHub Enterprise Server, it’s a nightmare.

It doesn’t support active-active. It only supports passive standbys. Minor version upgrades can’t be done without downtime, and don’t support rollbacks. If you deploy an update, and it has a bug, the only thing you can do is restore from backup leading to data loss.

This is the software they sell to their highest margin customers, and it fails even basic sniff tests of availability.

Data loss for source code is a really big deal.

Downtime for source control is a really big deal.

Anyone that would release such a product with a straight face, clearly doesn’t care deeply about availability.

So, the fact that their managed product is also having constant outages isn’t surprising.

I think the problem is that they just don’t care.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights concrete technical limitations in GitHub's enterprise product, which plausibly affect overall platform reliability.

comments.author: 827a
comments.text:
I don't want to give too much credit to Github, because their uptime is truly horrendous and they need to fix it. But: I've felt like its a little unfair to judge the uptime of company platforms like this; by saying "if any feature at all is down, its all down" and then translating that into 9s for the platform.

I never use Github Copilot; it does go down a lot, if their status page is to be believed; I don't really care when it goes down, because it going down doesn't bring down the rest of Github. I care about Github's uptime ignoring Copilot. Everyone's slice of what they care about is a little different, so the only correct way to speak on Github's uptime is to be precise and probably focus on a lot of the core stuff that tons of people care about and that's been struggling lately: Core git operations, website functionality, api access, actions, etc.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly points out the nuance in uptime metrics and the importance of focusing on critical services rather than aggregate numbers.

comments.author: mikeve
comments.text: Just to add a little bit of nuance to this not because I'm trying to defend GitHub, they definitely need to up their reliability, but the 90% uptime figure represents every single service that GitHub offers being online 90% of the time. You don't need every single service to be online in order to use GitHub. For example, I don't use Copilot myself and it's seen a 96.47% uptime, the worst of the services which are tracked.
comments.analysis: This adds nuance to uptime statistics, clarifying that not all service outages equally impact users, which is an important consideration.

comments.author: pilif
comments.text:
see also: https://thenewstack.io/github-will-prioritize-migrating-to-a...

A migration like this is a monumental undertaking to the level of where the only sensible way to do a migration like this is probably to not do it. I fully expect even worse reliability over the next few years before it'll get better.
comments.analysis: The comment realistically assesses the challenges of large-scale cloud migrations and their impact on service stability.

comments.author: Anon1096
comments.text: Anyone who used the phrase "measly" in relation to three nines is inadvertently admitting their lack of knowledge in massive systems. 99.9 and 99.95 is the target for some of the most common systems you use all day and is by no means easy to achieve. Even just relying on a couple regional AWS services will put your CEILING at three nines. It's even more embarrassing when people post that one GH uptime tracker that combines many services into 1 single number as if that means anything useful.
comments.analysis: This comment provides an informed perspective on uptime expectations and cautions against misinterpreting aggregated metrics.

comments.author: mosaibah
comments.text: the real problem isn't the reliability numbers, it's that GitHub sold itself as an integrated platform, every service you adopt raises the blast radius, teams that treat github like any other external dependency, with fallback runners and artifact mirrors, aren't sweating this
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully discusses architectural risks and best practices for mitigating dependency failures.

comments.author: Andrei_dev
comments.text: Our security scanning runs on GitHub Actions — every PR gets checked before merge. When GitHub goes down, the security gate goes down with it. PRs pile up, devs get impatient, start merging without waiting for checks. That's exactly when bad code gets through. And they keep throwing engineers at Copilot while the stuff that CI/CD actually depends on keeps falling over.
comments.analysis: The comment highlights a practical consequence of GitHub downtime on development workflows and code quality.

comments.author: neonihil
comments.text: Nothing unexpected. Microsoft has a remarkable talent for turning good products into useless ones. Skype is another good showcase of such talent.
comments.analysis: While somewhat emotional and anecdotal, this comment reflects a common perception about Microsoft's product stewardship.
